description:
You are acting as a monitoring-platform specialist who has to put in place the very first, absolutely minimal, “proof-of-concept” backup routine so that alert rules can later be attached to its results.

Your goal is to create a compressed backup of a small project directory, store an integrity checksum nearby, and append a one-line status record to a log file.  
Everything must happen inside the user’s home directory; you do NOT need elevated privileges.

PRE-EXISTING ITEMS  
1. Directory to back up:  
   /home/user/project  
   (At least one plain-text file such as /home/user/project/sample.txt is already there.)

TARGETS YOU MUST CREATE  
1. Backup directory (create it if it does not yet exist)  
   /home/user/backup  

2. Compressed archive of the whole project directory  
   /home/user/backup/project_backup.tar.gz  
   • It has to be a valid gzip-compressed tar archive containing the complete /home/user/project tree, starting at its top-level directory (i.e. the first entry shown by “tar -tzf” must be “project/”).  

3. SHA-256 checksum file for the archive  
   /home/user/backup/project_backup.tar.gz.sha256  
   • It must contain exactly one line, produced in the classic “shasum ‑a 256” format:  
     <64-hex-character-digest><two spaces>project_backup.tar.gz  
     (Note the *two* spaces before the file name and the trailing newline.)  

4. Human-readable status log that alerting will later tail  
   /home/user/backup/backup_status.log  
   • Append exactly ONE new line whose fields are separated by semicolons (;) in this precise order:  
     BACKUP_COMPLETED;FILE=project_backup.tar.gz;SIZE=<bytes>;SHA256=<64-hex-digest>  
     where  
     – <bytes> is the decimal size in bytes of project_backup.tar.gz (e.g. 12345)  
     – <64-hex-digest> is the same digest that appears in the .sha256 file  
   • No extra spaces are allowed in the line.  
   • Leave the rest of the file (any earlier lines) untouched.  

SUMMARY CHECKLIST (used by automated tests)  
After you finish, the tests will verify that:  
a) /home/user/backup exists.  
b) /home/user/backup/project_backup.tar.gz exists, is non-empty, and “tar -tzf” shows it begins with “project/”.  
c) /home/user/backup/project_backup.tar.gz.sha256 exists, contains one correctly-formatted line, and that the digest inside matches the actual SHA-256 of the archive.  
d) /home/user/backup/backup_status.log has a *new* final line that exactly follows the required semicolon-separated format and carries the same digest and byte-size found above.

You may use any standard GNU/Linux utilities that are normally available in a minimal distribution (tar, gzip, sha256sum, wc, printf/echo, etc.).  
The whole task can be accomplished with only 3-4 shell commands.  
Do NOT supply the exact commands here; the AI agent will decide how to execute them.
truth:
Initial on-disk state before the agent begins:

/home/user/project/               (directory, already present)
└── sample.txt                    (text file, arbitrary content)

No /home/user/backup directory or any of the target files exist yet.

Verification details relied upon by the automated grader:

1. Directory /home/user/backup must be created with default user permissions (0700 or 0755 acceptable).

2. Archive /home/user/backup/project_backup.tar.gz
   • Must be a valid gzip file (header bytes 1F 8B).
   • “tar -tzf project_backup.tar.gz | head -1” must output “project/”.
   • Size (stat -c%s) must be ≥ 1.

3. Checksum file /home/user/backup/project_backup.tar.gz.sha256
   • Must be exactly one line matching regex:
     ^[0-9a-f]{64}  project_backup\.tar\.gz\n$
   • Let DIGEST be the 64-hex string captured above.

4. Status log /home/user/backup/backup_status.log
   • The file must exist.
   • The very last line must match regex:
     ^BACKUP_COMPLETED;FILE=project_backup\.tar\.gz;SIZE=[1-9][0-9]*;SHA256=[0-9a-f]{64}$
   • The SIZE value must equal stat -c%s /home/user/backup/project_backup.tar.gz.
   • The SHA256 value must equal DIGEST obtained from the .sha256 file.

No other files or directories are required.
